design_philosophy.core_loop: Shoot descending enemy formations, collect ground capsules to build modular ship upgrades, toggle between unified and separated formation states for tactical advantage
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward between ship modularity (more firepower but larger target) versus separated formation (invulnerable pieces but exposed core)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Progressive transformation of a basic fighter into a customizable multi-part weapon system; culminating in brief invulnerable phoenix form
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unique modular powerup system where ship pieces attach as shields AND weapons, with tactical separation mechanic. Direct evolution of Moon Cresta that expands predecessor's concepts with innovative equipment variety (wave gun, double shot, etc.). Compared by critics to Super Mario Bros. for how it iterates on established series conventions while introducing distinctive mechanics

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: NES version adds customization of separated ship positions, giving players tactical control over formation layout

progression.upgrade_system: Modular ship pieces collected from destroyed ground capsules. Four different piece types provide unique weapons (wave gun, double shot, etc.). Pieces attach to core ship acting as both weapons and shields, absorbing hits before death. All four pieces enable phoenix transformation
progression.unlock_mechanism: Ground capsules must be shot down to unlock corresponding piece rewards; pieces progressively attach to growing ship
replayability_hooks: Strategic separation timing of ship modules
Experimenting with different piece loadout combinations
NES version's customizable formation positioning adds variation

world_building_techniques: Thematic naming: Wing Galibur fighter, Mandler army enemy faction
Japanese sci-fi mecha aesthetic evident in ship design and transformation mechanic
Phoenix invincibility form references mythological symbolism

danger_signals: Incoming enemy craft waves in formation
Ground-based enemy positions requiring tactical adaptation
environmental_storytelling: Ground capsule placement indicates strategic resource distribution
Varied enemy craft types suggest organized military hierarchy

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (Terra Cresta NES technical documentation and regional differences)
wikipedia - Terra Cresta (gameplay mechanics, design philosophy, reception analysis)
